title: Acidity and heartburn
excerpt: A calm, plain-language look at why acidity and heartburn happen, what Ayurveda sees behind that burning feeling, and a few everyday habits the tradition suggests you can start today.
body_html:
Why it happens

You ate something a little late, a little heavy, maybe a little too spicy, and now there is a sour, burning feeling rising up from your stomach into your chest and throat. Sometimes it comes with a sour or bitter taste in the mouth, a heaviness, or a tiredness that you did not earn. If this is you today, you are not alone, and you are not imagining it. This is one of the most common complaints there is, and there are gentle, sensible things you can do.

Ayurveda has a name for this kind of trouble: amlapitta, which simply means sour-pitta, or acid that has turned the wrong way. The classical text Madhava Nidana describes it almost exactly the way you feel it: indigestion, tiredness without effort, nausea, sour or bitter eructation (that acid burp), heaviness, and burning in the chest and throat. It even notes that it can travel upward or stay lower down. Reading a description written centuries ago that matches your morning is oddly reassuring: this is a known, understood pattern.

To understand why it happens, Ayurveda points to your digestion, which it calls agni, the inner fire that cooks your food. When that fire is steady, food is digested cleanly and you feel light and clear. The Caraka Samhita treats this agni as central to wellbeing, linking strength, nourishment and energy to its healthy working (Caraka Samhita, Chikitsasthana, Ch. 15). The same text says good, wholesome food is the very fuel on which this inner fire depends (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ch. 27).

When the fire runs irregular or too sharp, food is not cooked properly. The classics describe a sticky, undigested residue that can result, called ama. The Ashtanga Hridaya defines ama as raw, unprocessed material left behind when the digestive heat is too weak to finish the job (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 13). In plain terms, when digestion is off, the system gets clogged and irritated, and that sour, heavy, burning feeling is one way the body tells you so.

Amlapitta: literally sour-pitta, acid that has turned the wrong way.

What helps today

So what can you do today? The tradition leans heavily on simple food and rhythm rather than anything dramatic. Eat at regular times and try not to eat your heaviest meal late at night, since food sitting on a tired fire is exactly the situation the texts warn about (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ch. 27). Favour food that is warm, fresh and easy to digest over food that is very oily, very sour, or very spicy, which many people notice makes the burning worse. Cooling, soothing options like plain rice, moong dal, ripe sweet fruit, or a little cool (not iced) water often sit more kindly.

Ginger is the one herb the classical plant compendiums mention again and again for sluggish digestion. The Bhavaprakasha Nighantu places dried ginger (shunthi) among substances that kindle the digestive fire and help clear ama. A little fresh ginger with food is a long-standing household habit. Notice your own body, though: if your burning is already intense, strong or pungent things can aggravate it, so let how you feel be your guide.

For routine, give your stomach time. Try not to lie down right after eating, eat slowly and chew well, and leave a gap before bed. A calming practice helps too, because stress and rushed, anxious eating disturb digestion. A few minutes of slow, quiet breathing before meals, sitting upright and unhurried, settles the body into a state where it can actually digest. None of this is a quick fix. It is the steady, boring rhythm that the tradition trusts.

When to see a doctor

One honest note. If your acidity or heartburn is severe, keeps coming back, wakes you at night, comes with difficulty swallowing, vomiting, weight loss, black stools, or chest pain, please see a doctor or a qualified vaidya without delay. This article is general information drawn from classical sources, offered to support everyday habits, and it is not a diagnosis or a substitute for medical care.

Understanding Agni

The goal is a steady, balanced fire (sama). Too low leaves food undigested; too sharp burns through it. An irregular fire (vishama) swings between the two.

Try This Today

  • Keep dinner early and light: Food sitting on a tired fire late at night is exactly what the texts warn against.
  • Favour cooling foods: Plain rice, moong dal, ripe sweet fruit and cool (not iced) water.
  • Go easy on the triggers: Very oily, sour or spicy food often makes the burning worse.
  • Do not lie down after eating: Sit upright, eat slowly, and leave a gap before bed.
  • A calm pause before meals: A few slow breaths settle the body into a state where it can digest.

Key Ayurvedic Ingredients

Ginger (अदरक · शुण्ठी)

The nighantu texts class dried ginger as kindling to digestion. Use in moderation if the burning is already intense.

metafields.dek: A calm, plain-language look at why acidity and heartburn happen, what Ayurveda sees behind that burning feeling, and a few everyday habits the tradition suggests you can start today.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: A sour, burning feeling that rises from the stomach to the chest, often after late, heavy, oily or spicy meals.
Ayurveda calls it amlapitta and links it to an uneven digestive fire (agni) and the sticky residue (ama) it leaves behind.
What helps most: eat at regular times, keep dinner early and light, favour cooling foods, and do not lie down straight after eating.

glossary_terms.definition: Sour-pitta: the classical term for acidity and heartburn.
The fiery principle governing heat, digestion and metabolism.
The digestive fire that turns food into nourishment.
Sticky, undigested residue left when agni is weak.

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Amlapitta" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Amlapitta is defined as: Sour-pitta: the classical term for acidity and heartburn.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to keep dinner early and light. Food sitting on a tired fire late at night is exactly what the texts warn against.

faqs.question: What is the benefit of Ginger for this condition?
faqs.answer: According to classical texts, Ginger is highly regarded. The nighantu texts class dried ginger as kindling to digestion. Use in moderation if the burning is already intense.
